title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
Welcome!
This handbook introduces the Causal Roadmap, Targeted Maximum Likelihood Estimation (TMLE), and Super Learner for modern causal inference in epidemiology, with an emphasis on pharmacoepidemiology and clinical trial analysis. Each chapter builds on the previous ones, progressing from foundational concepts through estimation and diagnostics to longitudinal methods and advanced applications, with worked R code examples throughout.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.1 How to use this book
The chapters are organized into five parts plus appendices. Part I builds the conceptual foundations you need before running any estimator. Part II walks through each major estimation strategy with hands-on code. Part III covers diagnostics and treatment effect heterogeneity. Part IV tackles longitudinal settings with time-varying confounding. Part V addresses advanced applications including hybrid RCT-RWD designs, mediation, and time-to-event estimands. You can read the book front-to-back or jump to specific chapters as needed.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.2 Part I: Foundations
These chapters establish the conceptual framework for causal inference: why standard regression is not enough, how the Causal Roadmap structures an analysis, and how we formally link causal questions to quantities we can estimate from data.



Chapter
Description




1.1 Regression vs. Causal Inference
Why regression coefficients are not causal effects, and what the counterfactual framework offers instead.


1.2 The Causal Roadmap
The six-step Causal Roadmap for structuring any causal analysis, starting with the target trial protocol.


1.3 Identification and Estimands
Formal identification assumptions (consistency, exchangeability, positivity) and how to translate causal parameters into statistical estimands.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.3 Part II: Core Estimators
These chapters cover the core estimation approaches for causal inference, from outcome modeling to doubly robust methods, with progressive worked examples in R.



Chapter
Description




2.1 G-Computation
Outcome modeling and standardization: fit a model for \(E[Y \mid A, W]\), predict under interventions, and average to get marginal causal effects.


2.2 Inverse Probability of Treatment Weighting
Propensity-score-based estimation: reweight the sample to create a pseudo-population where treatment is independent of confounders. Covers stabilized weights, truncation, and diagnostics.


2.3 Doubly Robust Estimation and TMLE
AIPW and TMLE side by side: why combining outcome and treatment models gives you two chances to get it right, with efficient influence curve-based inference.


2.4 From Question to Estimate: TMLE in Practice
A progressive, illustrated walkthrough comparing naive, g-computation, IPTW, AIPW, and TMLE estimators on a single cardiovascular safety dataset.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.4 Part III: Diagnostics and Heterogeneity
These chapters address how to assess whether your analysis is trustworthy and how to explore treatment effect variation across patient subgroups.



Chapter
Description




3.4 Effect Modification
Conditional average treatment effects (CATE), TMLE-based subgroup analyses, and causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effect estimation.


3.5 Advanced Diagnostics
A systematic framework for identifiability, nuisance model, estimator, and sensitivity diagnostics, with executable R examples.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.5 Part IV: Longitudinal Causal Inference
These chapters extend the core methods to longitudinal settings where treatment, confounders, and censoring co-evolve over time.



Chapter
Description




3.7 Imperfect Adherence and Time-Varying Confounding
Why conditioning on time-varying adherence biases causal effects, and how longitudinal TMLE and LMTP address treatment-confounder feedback.


3.9 An Illustrated Guide to Longitudinal TMLE
A visual, step-by-step guide to L-TMLE: backwards sequential regression, cumulative treatment probabilities, and time-step-specific targeting.


3.3 Longitudinal Case Study
A worked case study applying the Causal Roadmap to a longitudinal pharmacoepidemiology setting with time-varying confounding.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.6 Part V: Advanced Applications
These chapters tackle specialized settings: hybrid RCT-RWD designs, pre-specified regulatory analyses, mediation decomposition, and time-to-event estimands.



Chapter
Description




3.6 RWD Meets RCT: Hybrid Designs
External control arms, transportability, ES-CV-TMLE, and A-TMLE for combining randomized and real-world data sources.


3.8 TMLE in the Clean-Room Framework
Pre-specified TMLE analysis for regulatory pharmacoepidemiology: locked analysis plans, reproducible scripts, and audit trails.


3.10 TMLE for Mediation Analysis
Decomposing total effects into direct and indirect pathways using TMLE-based mediation with the crumble package.


3.11 Estimands in Time-to-Event Safety Analyses
How ICH E9(R1) estimand strategies shape what a safety analysis measures, with simulation-based comparisons of KM, IPCW, and TMLE estimators.
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

Common Pitfalls
A catalog of the most frequent mistakes in causal inference and how to avoid them.


Annotated Bibliography
Key readings in causal inference, targeted learning, and real-world evidence generation.


Other Resources

title: Causal Learning Handbook
text:
0.8 Package installations
To run the code examples in this book, install the following R packages:

## Core CRAN packages
cran_pkgs <- c(
  "tidyverse", "here", "sandwich", "lmtest", "Matrix", "survival"
)

## Super Learner ecosystem
sl_pkgs <- c(
  "SuperLearner", "glmnet", "gam", "ranger", "randomForest", "xgboost"
)

## Targeted Learning and causal inference
causal_pkgs <- c(
  "tmle", "ltmle", "lmtp", "hal9001", "origami"
)

## Diagnostics and sensitivity analysis
diagnostic_pkgs <- c("EValue", "episensr", "causalsens")

## Combine and install missing packages
all_pkgs <- unique(c(cran_pkgs, sl_pkgs, causal_pkgs, diagnostic_pkgs))
installed <- rownames(installed.packages())
to_install <- setdiff(all_pkgs, installed)

if (length(to_install) > 0) {
  install.packages(to_install, dependencies = TRUE)
}
crumbs: 1  Causal Learning Handbook

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.1 Why Not Just Use Regression?
Suppose you want to know whether patients who start denosumab have a higher three-year risk of heart attack than those who start zoledronic acid. You have a large claims database. The natural instinct is to fit a logistic regression, adjust for confounders, and interpret the treatment coefficient as a causal effect. That instinct is wrong, and the consequences can mislead clinical decisions.
The problem is not that regression is useless. The problem is that a regression coefficient answers a different question than the one you are asking. The adjusted log-odds ratio from logistic regression is a conditional measure on the wrong scale, entangled with model assumptions that are rarely examined. Confounding, non-collapsibility, and misspecification can each silently distort the number you report.
This chapter is the canonical reference for why regression coefficients are not causal effects. We walk through a concrete example, show exactly where naive and adjusted regression go wrong, introduce the counterfactual framework that defines what a causal effect actually is, and demonstrate g-computation as a first alternative. Later chapters will reference back to this discussion rather than repeat it. The chapter proceeds as follows:
Causal conclusions require identification assumptions (consistency, exchangeability, positivity) that no statistical method can verify from data alone.
Multivariable regression has long been the standard tool in observational research for “adjusting” for confounders. But regression coefficients don’t always represent causal effects. In fact, they almost never do without additional assumptions and deliberate analytic choices that go beyond what standard regression provides.
Key point. If you’ve been trained to interpret an adjusted regression coefficient as “the effect of X on Y, holding all else equal,” you’re in good company, but that interpretation quietly smuggles in causal language that the model itself cannot justify. The coefficient tells you about the conditional association within strata defined by the other variables in the model. Whether that association equals a causal effect depends on assumptions that regression alone cannot verify.
Let’s consider a motivating example based on a real-world study comparing two osteoporosis treatments: denosumab (Prolia) and zoledronic acid.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.1.1 Motivating Example: Comparing Osteoporosis Treatments
Imagine we’re interested in whether patients who initiate denosumab have higher 3-year risk of heart attack or stroke compared to those initiating zoledronic acid.
We might be tempted to run the following:

# simulate crude comparison (not real data)
set.seed(123)
n <- 2000
treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) # 1 = denosumab, 0 = zoledronic acid
age <- rnorm(n, 75, 6)
cvd_history <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.1 * (age - 70)))

# outcome depends on age and history
risk <- plogis(-2 + 0.4 * treatment + 0.05 * (age - 70) + 1 * cvd_history)
outcome <- rbinom(n, 1, risk)

model1 <- glm(outcome ~ treatment, family = binomial)
summary(model1)
#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = outcome ~ treatment, family = binomial)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept) -1.05939    0.07197 -14.720  < 2e-16 ***
#> treatment    0.33425    0.09887   3.381 0.000723 ***
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 2414.0  on 1999  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 2402.5  on 1998  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 2406.5
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4







What the Crude Estimate Conflates



The crude coefficient bundles together any true treatment effect with the influence of confounders (age, CVD history). You cannot disentangle these from an unadjusted regression. This is confounding bias, and as we will see, adjustment alone is not enough.




2.1.2 Adjusting for Confounders
We add adjustment:

model2 <- glm(outcome ~ treatment + age + cvd_history, family = binomial)
summary(model2)
#> 
#> Call:
#> glm(formula = outcome ~ treatment + age + cvd_history, family = binomial)
#> 
#> Coefficients:
#>              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
#> (Intercept) -6.398207   0.694981  -9.206  < 2e-16 ***
#> treatment    0.366412   0.103506   3.540    4e-04 ***
#> age          0.060479   0.009213   6.564 5.22e-11 ***
#> cvd_history  1.090838   0.121918   8.947  < 2e-16 ***
#> ---
#> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
#> 
#> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)
#> 
#>     Null deviance: 2414.0  on 1999  degrees of freedom
#> Residual deviance: 2223.1  on 1996  degrees of freedom
#> AIC: 2231.1
#> 
#> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4







The Adjusted Coefficient Is Still NOT a Causal Effect



Even after adjustment, the logistic regression coefficient is a conditional log-odds ratio, not a marginal causal effect. The odds ratio is non-collapsible, lives on a different scale from the risk difference, and inherits every parametric assumption in the model.


Key point. Adjustment gets you closer, but the regression coefficient from a logistic model is not, in general, the average treatment effect you are looking for.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.2 Enter the Counterfactual Framework






Defining Causal Effects with Potential Outcomes



For each person we imagine two hypothetical worlds: Y(1) (outcome under denosumab) and Y(0) (outcome under zoledronic acid). The average treatment effect (ATE) is:
\[
ATE = E[Y(1) - Y(0)]
\]


The fundamental problem of causal inference is that each person only receives one treatment. We observe Y(1) or Y(0), never both. Under three key identification assumptions, exchangeability (no unmeasured confounding), consistency (the observed outcome under the treatment actually received equals the potential outcome), and positivity (every individual has a nonzero probability of receiving either treatment given their covariates), we can identify the causal ATE from observed data using the g-computation formula:
\[
ATE = E_W\big[ E[Y \mid A = 1, W] - E[Y \mid A = 0, W] \big]
\]
where W represents the measured confounders and A is the treatment. This expression says: within each confounder stratum, compute the difference in expected outcomes between treated and untreated, then average over the distribution of confounders in the population.
This formula motivates standardization (g-computation), inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW), and targeted minimum loss-based estimation (TMLE), all of which are designed to estimate this population-level causal quantity.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.3 When Regression Fails






Model Misspecification Can Silently Bias Your Results



Misspecification bias is silent. A regression with the wrong functional form still produces coefficients and p-values with no warning. This motivates semiparametric estimators (like TMLE with machine learning) that relax parametric assumptions.


Let’s compare regression to a nonparametric substitution estimator (g-computation):

# Fit model
model3 <- glm(outcome ~ treatment + age + cvd_history, family = binomial)

# Predict counterfactual outcomes
newdata1 <- data.frame(treatment = 1, age = age, cvd_history = cvd_history)
p1 <- predict(model3, newdata = newdata1, type = "response")

newdata0 <- data.frame(treatment = 0, age = age, cvd_history = cvd_history)
p0 <- predict(model3, newdata = newdata0, type = "response")

# Estimate marginal risk difference
mean(p1 - p0)  # this is g-computation
#> [1] 0.06897059







Why G-Computation Gives You a Population-Level Causal Effect



G-computation predicts counterfactual outcomes for every person under treatment and under control, then averages the difference. The result is a marginal risk difference, a population-level quantity directly relevant to decision-making, unlike the conditional log-odds ratio from regression.


Compare this to the regression coefficient:

coef(model3)["treatment"]
#> treatment 
#> 0.3664119

The coefficient gives you a conditional odds ratio, but the g-comp version gives you a marginal risk difference, a more interpretable, population-level quantity.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.4 Summary






Key Takeaways




Regression is not inherently causal. It estimates conditional associations under strong model assumptions. An adjusted coefficient is not the same as a causal effect.
Causal inference starts with a question, not a model. Define your causal question and target estimand (e.g., the ATE on the risk difference scale) before choosing an analytic method.
The counterfactual framework clarifies what we want to estimate. Y(1), Y(0), and the ATE give us precise, model-free definitions of causal effects.
G-computation, IPTW, and TMLE estimate marginal causal effects. These methods target the population-level quantity defined by the g-computation formula, rather than relying on regression coefficients that conflate the estimand with the model.
The conditional log-odds ratio from logistic regression is not the ATE. This is perhaps the single most important distinction for epidemiologists moving from traditional regression to causal inference methods.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.5 What comes next
Now that you understand why regression coefficients are not causal effects, the next chapter introduces the Causal Roadmap: a structured workflow that turns a scientific question into a defensible analysis plan, from question definition through estimation and interpretation.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.6 Check your understanding


Q1. Why can’t you interpret an adjusted logistic regression coefficient as the ATE?

The adjusted coefficient from logistic regression is a conditional log-odds ratio. It describes the association between treatment and outcome within strata defined by the covariates in the model. This differs from the ATE (a marginal risk difference) in at least two ways. First, the odds ratio is non-collapsible: even without confounding, a conditional odds ratio and a marginal odds ratio will differ, so you cannot read the coefficient as a population-level summary. Second, the coefficient lives on the log-odds scale, not the risk-difference scale, and converting between them requires averaging over the covariate distribution (which is exactly what g-computation does). Finally, the coefficient inherits every parametric assumption in the model (log-linearity, no interactions, correct link function); if any assumption fails, the coefficient is biased for any causal quantity.



Q2. What are the three identification assumptions needed to give the g-computation formula a causal interpretation?


Exchangeability (no unmeasured confounding): Within levels of the measured covariates W, treatment assignment is independent of the potential outcomes, \(Y(a) \perp A \mid W\). This means every common cause of treatment and outcome has been measured and adjusted for.
Consistency: The observed outcome for a person who received treatment \(a\) equals their potential outcome \(Y(a)\). This requires a well-defined intervention with no hidden variations.
Positivity: Every covariate stratum has a nonzero probability of receiving each treatment level, \(P(A = a \mid W = w) > 0\) for all relevant \(a\) and \(w\). Without positivity, there are subgroups for which no data-based comparison is possible.

If any of these assumptions fails, the g-computation estimator may be biased regardless of how well the outcome model fits the data.



Q3. What is the difference between a conditional log-odds ratio and a marginal risk difference?

A conditional log-odds ratio is the change in the log-odds of the outcome per unit change in treatment, holding all other covariates fixed at specific values. It is a stratum-specific measure on the odds scale. A marginal risk difference is the difference in the probability of the outcome if everyone in the population were treated versus if everyone were untreated. It is a population-averaged measure on the probability scale. In nonlinear models (such as logistic regression), these two quantities answer different questions and are generally not numerically equal, even when the model is correctly specified. The marginal risk difference is usually more directly relevant for public health decision-making because it tells you how many additional cases per 100 people would result from a policy change.

2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses
text:
2.8 Software Implementation (R)
This example uses the tmle package to estimate the average treatment effect (ATE) for a point treatment, and contrasts the result with a naive regression coefficient.

Simulate a simple confounded dataset: \(W\) causes both \(A\) and \(Y\)
Naive regression of \(Y\) on \(A\) (ignoring confounding) yields a biased coefficient
The tmle package estimates the ATE using outcome modeling + targeting
Compare: the naive coefficient ≠ the causal ATE


set.seed(1)
n <- 500
W <- rnorm(n)
A <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.5 * W))
Y <- 0.8 * A + 1.2 * W + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)  # true ATE = 0.8

## -- Naive regression (biased) --
naive_fit <- lm(Y ~ A)
cat("Naive regression coefficient on A:", round(coef(naive_fit)["A"], 3), "\n")

## -- TMLE (targets the ATE) --
if (requireNamespace("tmle", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(tmle)
  tmle_fit <- tmle(
    Y = Y, A = A,
    W = data.frame(W = W),
    Q.SL.library = "SL.glm",
    g.SL.library  = "SL.glm"
  )
  cat("TMLE ATE estimate:", round(tmle_fit$estimates$ATE$psi, 3), "\n")
  cat("95% CI:", round(tmle_fit$estimates$ATE$CI, 3), "\n")
} else {
  message("Install the 'tmle' package:  install.packages('tmle')")
}
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
2  Chapter 1.1: Limitations of Standard Regression Analyses

title: 3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap
text:
3.0.1 Why Use a Causal Roadmap?
In this chapter, we introduce the Causal Roadmap, a structured approach for asking and answering causal questions with observational data. We begin with the target trial protocol, which forces you to define the scientific question precisely before choosing any statistical method, and then walk through the six steps of the causal roadmap that translate that question into a defensible analysis.
Causal conclusions require identification assumptions and diagnostic evidence that the data support the target estimand.
In epidemiology, it is tempting to jump straight to fitting a model: “Let’s run a regression and see what we find.” But this approach conflates the scientific question with the statistical procedure, making it difficult to evaluate whether the analysis actually answers the question you care about. Modern causal inference begins by asking: What is the causal question we are trying to answer?
Key point. A causal roadmap ensures transparency in how evidence is generated, separation of scientific questions from statistical methods, clear articulation of assumptions, and structured thinking in both trial and non-randomized settings. Starting with a well-defined question, before touching data or code, is the single most important step in any causal analysis.
We’ll walk step-by-step through each part of the roadmap, with illustrative examples.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap

title: 3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap
text:
3.1 A Simple Simulated Example
We’ll simulate a small dataset to show how steps 1 through 5 look in practice.

set.seed(42)
n <- 1000
age <- rnorm(n, 75, 6)
cvd_history <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.1 * (age - 70)))
W <- data.frame(age, cvd_history)
A <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-1 + 0.05 * (age - 70) + 1.5 * cvd_history))
Y <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-2 + 0.4 * A + 0.1 * (age - 70) + 1 * cvd_history))
data <- data.frame(W, A, Y)

Estimate ATE using g-computation:

model <- glm(Y ~ A + age + cvd_history, family = binomial, data = data)
newdata1 <- transform(data, A = 1)
newdata0 <- transform(data, A = 0)
p1 <- predict(model, newdata = newdata1, type = "response")
p0 <- predict(model, newdata = newdata0, type = "response")
mean(p1 - p0)
#> [1] 0.1206902







Interpreting the G-Computation Result



The number produced by mean(p1 - p0) is the estimated average causal risk difference. In plain language: it tells you how much the probability of the outcome (e.g., a cardiovascular event) would change, on average, if everyone in the study population had received treatment (A = 1) compared to if everyone had received no treatment (A = 0). For example, a value of 0.07 means that treatment is estimated to increase the risk of the outcome by 7 percentage points on average across the population, after adjusting for age and CVD history. This is the core quantity that g-computation targets, a population-level “what if” comparison.








Summary



The Causal Roadmap gives a rigorous framework for designing and evaluating real-world effect estimates. Before choosing a statistical method, start with the science: define your causal question, specify your model and assumptions, decide how to express the effect, and choose a transparent, defensible method.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap

title: 3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap
text:
3.2 Check your understanding


Q1. Why must the causal question be defined before choosing a statistical method?

The causal question determines everything downstream: which variables are confounders (and therefore must be adjusted for), what the target estimand is (e.g., ATE on the risk difference scale vs. a conditional odds ratio), and which assumptions are required for identification. If you choose a statistical method first, say, logistic regression, you have implicitly committed to a conditional odds ratio as your estimand, which may not match the scientific question you actually care about. Starting with the question also prevents “researcher degrees of freedom”: when the estimand is pre-specified, there is less temptation to explore multiple model specifications and selectively report favorable results.



Q2. What is the difference between a causal estimand and a statistical estimand?

A causal estimand is defined in terms of potential outcomes, for example, \(E[Y(1) - Y(0)]\), the average treatment effect. It describes a contrast between hypothetical worlds and cannot be directly computed from observed data without assumptions. A statistical estimand is a function of the observed data distribution, for example, \(E_W[E[Y \mid A=1, W] - E[Y \mid A=0, W]]\). The identification step of the Causal Roadmap shows that, under specific assumptions (exchangeability, positivity, consistency), the causal estimand equals a particular statistical estimand. Without those assumptions, the statistical quantity you compute may have no causal interpretation at all.



Q3. Can covariate balance checks prove that exchangeability holds?

No. Covariate balance checks (e.g., standardized mean differences after weighting) can only assess whether measured covariates are balanced between treatment groups. Exchangeability requires that there are no unmeasured common causes of treatment and outcome, a condition that depends on subject-matter knowledge and cannot be verified from the data. Good balance on observed covariates is necessary but not sufficient: an unmeasured confounder can induce bias even when every measured covariate is perfectly balanced. Balance checks are a useful diagnostic, but they should never be interpreted as evidence that the exchangeability assumption holds.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap

title: 3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap
text:
3.3 What comes next
With the causal question defined and the roadmap in hand, the next chapter formalizes the identification step: the assumptions needed to bridge potential outcomes and observed data, and the statistical estimands that result.

3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap

title: 3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap
text:
3.5 Software Implementation (R)
This example maps the six-step Causal Roadmap to a single tmle call. Each comment below labels the roadmap step that the corresponding code addresses.

Step 1 (Question): Average treatment effect of a binary exposure on a continuous outcome
Step 2 (Causal model): Observed data \((W, A, Y)\); \(W\) confounds \(A \to Y\)
Step 3 (Assumptions): Consistency, exchangeability given \(W\), positivity
Step 4 (Estimand): \(\psi = E[E(Y \mid A=1, W)] - E[E(Y \mid A=0, W)]\)
Steps 5 to 6 (Estimation + Inference): TMLE with Super Learner for \(Q\) and \(g\)


set.seed(1)
n <- 500

## -- Step 2: Simulate from a causal model --
W1 <- rnorm(n)
W2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.4)
A  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.5 + 0.6 * W1 + 0.3 * W2))
Y  <- A + 0.8 * W1 - 0.5 * W2 + rnorm(n)  # true ATE = 1.0

## -- Steps 5 to 6: Estimation and inference via TMLE --
if (requireNamespace("tmle", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(tmle)
  tmle_fit <- tmle(
    Y = Y, A = A,
    W = data.frame(W1 = W1, W2 = W2),
    Q.SL.library = c("SL.glm", "SL.mean"),
    g.SL.library  = c("SL.glm", "SL.mean")
  )
  cat("Roadmap Step 5 - TMLE ATE:", round(tmle_fit$estimates$ATE$psi, 3), "\n")
  cat("Roadmap Step 6 - 95% CI: ", round(tmle_fit$estimates$ATE$CI, 3), "\n")
} else {
  message("Install the 'tmle' package:  install.packages('tmle')")
}
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
3  Chapter 1.2: The Causal Roadmap

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
4.1 Why this chapter matters: the hormone therapy paradox
In this chapter, we connect the causal estimand, the quantity that answers our scientific question, to a statistical estimand, which is something we can estimate from observed data. This is the crucial middle step of the Causal Roadmap: translating what we want to know into what we can learn from the dataset at hand.
We will walk carefully through: - Identification: when causal effects are estimable from data - Statistical estimands: mapping causal parameters to observable quantities - Positivity, consistency, and exchangeability in practice - Why regression coefficients are not causal effects (see Chapter 1.1 for the full treatment) - How to compute identified estimands using real R code
A classic reason epidemiologists care so much about identification is the hormone therapy paradox. For years, observational studies suggested that postmenopausal hormone therapy appeared protective against coronary heart disease. The association looked reassuring and even clinically plausible. But later randomized trial evidence did not support the same simple protective interpretation.
What went wrong? The key lesson is not that observational data are useless. The lesson is that an observed association is not automatically the causal effect we want. Women who initiated hormone therapy often differed from nonusers in ways that also predicted cardiovascular risk. They may have had different healthcare access, preventive care, health behaviors, socioeconomic resources, or baseline risk profiles. Once that possibility is on the table, the question becomes:
That is the central job of this chapter. Before we estimate anything, we need to know whether our causal estimand can be linked to the observed data under assumptions we are willing to defend. The hormone therapy story is a reminder that strong methods begin before modeling, at the stage of defining the estimand, defending exchangeability, and checking whether identification is even plausible.
The following simulation makes this concrete. A single binary confounder is enough to make a crude comparison misleading, while g-computation (which implements the identification formula) recovers the true causal effect.
set.seed(42)
n <- 2000
W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)                    # confounder: e.g., prior CVD
A <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.5 + 1.5 * W)) # treatment depends on W
Y <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-2 + 0.3 * A + 1.5 * W))  # true causal effect of A is modest

# Crude comparison (confounded)
crude_rd <- mean(Y[A == 1]) - mean(Y[A == 0])

# G-computation (adjusting for W)
mod <- glm(Y ~ A + W, family = binomial)
p1 <- predict(mod, newdata = data.frame(A = 1, W = W), type = "response")
p0 <- predict(mod, newdata = data.frame(A = 0, W = W), type = "response")
gcomp_ate <- mean(p1 - p0)

cat("Crude risk difference (confounded):", round(crude_rd, 3), "\n")

Crude risk difference (confounded): 0.133 

cat("G-computation ATE (adjusted):      ", round(gcomp_ate, 3), "\n")

G-computation ATE (adjusted):       0.051
The crude estimate overstates the effect because people with prior CVD (W = 1) are more likely to receive treatment and more likely to have the outcome. G-computation breaks this confounding by standardizing over W.
Causal conclusions require identification assumptions and diagnostic evidence that the data support the target estimand.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
Did the observed comparison identify the causal effect, or did it identify only an association shaped by confounding and selection?











Learning objectives




Define identification and explain why it is necessary before estimation
State the three core identification assumptions: consistency, exchangeability, and positivity
Distinguish a causal estimand (defined via potential outcomes) from a statistical estimand (a function of the observed-data distribution)
Derive the g-computation identification formula from the assumptions
Recognize when identification fails and what alternative strategies exist (sensitivity analysis, bounding, instrumental variables)
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
10.1 6.1 Overlap check






Diagnosing Positivity Violations in Practice



The propensity score plot below is your primary diagnostic. Look for:

Mass piling up near 0 or 1: Some patients have near-certain probability of receiving (or not receiving) treatment. The data contain little information about what would happen under the other treatment.
Poor overlap between groups: If the propensity score distributions for \(A=1\) and \(A=0\) lack common support, causal estimates in the non-overlapping region rely on extrapolation.

When you see these patterns, consider restricting to the region of common support, trimming extreme weights, or acknowledging the limitations.



plot(density(predict(glm(A ~ age + cvd, family = binomial, data = dat), type = "response")),
     main = "Propensity Score Overlap", xlab = "Estimated Propensity Score")








ps <- predict(glm(A ~ age + cvd, family = binomial, data = dat), type = "response")
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
12.1 Check your understanding


Q1. If you have perfect covariate balance between treated and untreated groups, does that prove exchangeability holds?

No. Covariate balance on measured covariates is necessary but not sufficient for exchangeability. Exchangeability requires that there are no unmeasured common causes of treatment and outcome, a condition that cannot be verified from the data alone. You could have perfect balance on every variable in your dataset while an unmeasured confounder (e.g., frailty, physician preference, or disease severity not captured in the data) still biases the estimate. Balance checks are useful diagnostics, but they cannot substitute for substantive knowledge and a well-reasoned DAG.



Q2. A colleague runs a logistic regression and reports the coefficient on treatment as “the causal effect.” What is wrong with this interpretation?

A logistic regression coefficient is a conditional log-odds ratio. It describes the association between treatment and outcome holding covariates fixed at specific values. This is not a causal effect for at least two reasons. First, a coefficient from a regression model is only a causal effect if the identification assumptions (consistency, exchangeability, positivity) hold and the model is correctly specified. Simply running a regression does not guarantee either. Second, the conditional log-odds ratio is a different quantity from the marginal risk difference (ATE) that typically answers the scientific question. The log-odds ratio is non-collapsible: its value changes depending on which covariates are conditioned on, even absent confounding. To obtain a causal estimate on a meaningful scale, you need to go through the identification step and apply a method like g-computation that standardizes over the population. See Chapter 1.1 for a detailed demonstration of this distinction.



Q3. What is a positivity violation, and what should you do when you encounter one?

A positivity violation occurs when certain covariate strata have zero (or near-zero) probability of receiving one of the treatment levels. Structural violations arise when some patients are never treated by clinical convention (e.g., very elderly patients never receiving aggressive chemotherapy). The causal effect is simply not defined in those strata, and no statistical method can fix this. Practical (random) violations occur when some strata have very few patients on one treatment, leading to propensity scores near 0 or 1 and unstable estimates. When you encounter positivity violations, you should: (1) inspect propensity score distributions for mass near the boundaries, (2) consider restricting the target population to the region of overlap where the effect is well-defined, (3) use weight trimming or stabilized weights if using IPTW, and (4) be transparent about the limitations and the population to which your estimate applies.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
12.2 What comes next
Before moving to estimation, the next chapter introduces target trial emulation: the discipline of designing every observational analysis as if it were a randomized trial. Target trial thinking prevents common biases (immortal time bias, misaligned time zero, vague comparators) and connects the causal roadmap directly to real-world evidence practice. After that, Part II turns to estimation methods.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models
text:
12.4 Software Implementation (R)
This example estimates the effect of a modified treatment policy (a stochastic intervention that shifts treatment probability) using the lmtp package. This connects directly to the identification results in this chapter: rather than asking “what if everyone were treated?”, we ask “what if each person’s probability of treatment increased?”

Simulate panel data with a single time point
Use lmtp_tmle() to estimate the effect of shifting the treatment mechanism
Falls back to tmle for a standard ATE if lmtp is unavailable


set.seed(1)
n <- 500
W1 <- rnorm(n)
W2 <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5)
A  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-0.3 + 0.5 * W1))
Y  <- as.numeric(rbinom(n, 1, plogis(-1 + 0.8 * A + 0.6 * W1 + 0.3 * W2)))

if (requireNamespace("lmtp", quietly = TRUE)) {
  library(lmtp)

  dat <- data.frame(W1 = W1, W2 = W2, A = A, Y = Y)

  ## Modified treatment policy: shift everyone to A = 1
  result <- lmtp_tmle(
    data = dat,
    trt = "A",
    outcome = "Y",
    baseline = c("W1", "W2"),
    shift = function(data, trt) rep(1, nrow(data)),
    outcome_type = "binomial",
    learners_outcome = "SL.glm",
    learners_trt = "SL.glm",
    folds = 5
  )
  print(result)
} else if (requireNamespace("tmle", quietly = TRUE)) {
  message("lmtp not available; falling back to tmle for a standard ATE.")
  library(tmle)
  fit <- tmle(Y = Y, A = A, W = data.frame(W1, W2),
              Q.SL.library = "SL.glm", g.SL.library = "SL.glm")
  cat("ATE:", round(fit$estimates$ATE$psi, 3), "\n")
  cat("95% CI:", round(fit$estimates$ATE$CI, 3), "\n")
} else {
  message("Install 'lmtp' or 'tmle':
    install.packages('lmtp')
    install.packages('tmle')")
}
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
4  Chapter 1.3: From Causal Questions to Analysis: Identification, Estimands, and Statistical Models

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.1 A motivating disaster: immortal time bias in statin studies
In this chapter, we formalize target trial emulation, the practice of designing every observational analysis as if it were emulating a specific, well-defined randomized trial. We will see how this discipline prevents some of the most common (and most damaging) biases in real-world evidence, and how it connects to the Causal Roadmap introduced in Chapter 1.2 and the identification framework in Chapter 1.3.
Designing observational analyses as if they were trials.
In 2006, several observational studies reported that statin users had substantially lower mortality than non-users, not just from cardiovascular disease, but from cancer, trauma, and even infections. The effect sizes were implausibly large. Were statins a miracle drug?
No. The problem was immortal time bias, a direct consequence of failing to align time zero with treatment assignment. Here is what happened:
The result was a spurious survival advantage for statin users that had nothing to do with the drug. This bias can be enormous, sometimes accounting for the entire observed effect.
This chapter teaches you to avoid this class of mistakes by starting every analysis with a target trial protocol. We will walk through each protocol component, show how common biases map to protocol violations, simulate immortal time bias so you can see it yourself, and then work through a complete example.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
Researchers identified patients who filled a statin prescription at some point during follow-up.
They classified the entire follow-up period for these patients as “statin exposed,” including the time before the prescription was filled.
During that early period, the patient could not have died (otherwise they would never have filled the prescription). That time was, by definition, “immortal.”
Non-users had no such guaranteed survival period.








The core lesson



Immortal time bias arises when time zero (the start of follow-up) is not aligned with the moment of treatment assignment. A target trial protocol forces you to define time zero explicitly, preventing this error before any data are analyzed.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
section: 5.2 1. Why Observational Analyses Should Start by Emulating a Target Trial
text:
5.2 1. Why Observational Analyses Should Start by Emulating a Target Trial

5.2.1 The gap between what we want and what we have
When we ask a causal question (“Does initiating statins reduce the 5-year risk of major adverse cardiovascular events?”), the gold standard answer would come from a randomized controlled trial (RCT). The trial protocol would specify who is eligible, when follow-up begins, what interventions are compared, and how outcomes are measured. Randomization would handle confounding. The protocol would be written before enrollment begins.
With observational data, we have none of these guarantees. Treatment is not randomized. Eligibility is defined post hoc. Time zero is ambiguous. The comparison group may be poorly defined. Without discipline, the analyst can inadvertently answer a question that differs from the one they intended, or, worse, answer a question that has no coherent causal interpretation at all.


5.2.2 Target trial emulation as a bridge
Target trial emulation (Hernan and Robins, 2016) provides that discipline. The idea is straightforward:

Specify the protocol for the ideal randomized trial you would like to run.
Emulate each protocol component using the observational data you actually have.
Document each deviation between the ideal trial and the emulation, and assess how each deviation could introduce bias.

This is not a statistical method. It is a design framework, a checklist that forces you to make explicit decisions that might otherwise remain implicit (and wrong). As we discussed in Chapter 1.2, the target trial protocol is the starting point for Step 1 of the Causal Roadmap: defining the causal question.






Think of it this way



A target trial protocol is to an observational study what an architectural blueprint is to a building. You would never start construction without a blueprint. You should never start an observational causal analysis without a target trial protocol.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.3 2. The Target Trial Protocol Components
A complete target trial protocol has eight components. Each one must be specified before any data manipulation or modeling begins.

5.3.1 2.1 Eligibility criteria
What it means: Who would be enrolled in the trial?
Eligibility criteria define the population to which causal conclusions will apply. In observational emulations, this translates directly to cohort inclusion and exclusion criteria applied at time zero.
Example: Adults aged 40 to 75 with newly diagnosed hyperlipidemia, no prior cardiovascular event, no prior statin use, and no contraindication to statins.


5.3.2 2.2 Treatment strategies
What it means: What interventions are being compared?
You must define at least two well-specified treatment strategies. Vague strategies (“statin use” vs. “no statin use”) lead to vague causal questions. Precise strategies specify the drug, dose, timing, and adherence expectations.
Example: (A) Initiate atorvastatin within 30 days of diagnosis and remain on therapy for at least 2 years. (B) Do not initiate any statin within 30 days of diagnosis.


5.3.3 2.3 Assignment procedure
What it means: How would participants be assigned to treatment strategies?
In the target trial, this would be randomization. In the emulation, we observe natural treatment decisions and use statistical methods to adjust for confounding. This component reminds us that we need the exchangeability assumption (Chapter 1.3) and must identify all important confounders.


5.3.4 2.4 Time zero
What it means: When does follow-up begin?
This is the single most important component for preventing bias. Time zero must simultaneously satisfy three conditions:

The participant meets the eligibility criteria
The participant is assigned to (or begins) a treatment strategy
Follow-up for the outcome begins

When these three events do not coincide, biases arise. Immortal time bias, as we saw above, is a direct consequence of time zero misalignment.






Time zero alignment is non-negotiable



If eligibility, treatment assignment, and the start of follow-up do not all occur at the same moment, your analysis will be biased. There are no statistical fixes for a misaligned time zero. The only fix is to redesign the study.




5.3.5 2.5 Outcome
What it means: What endpoint is being measured?
The outcome must be clearly defined and measured the same way across treatment groups. In observational settings, you must consider whether outcome ascertainment differs by treatment group (for example, patients on statins may have more frequent lab tests, leading to earlier detection of some outcomes).


5.3.6 2.6 Follow-up period
What it means: How long are participants followed?
The follow-up period begins at time zero and ends at the outcome, death, loss to follow-up, or the administrative end of the study, whichever comes first. Specifying this in advance prevents data-driven choices about follow-up length.


5.3.7 2.7 Causal contrast (estimand)
What it means: What summary measure compares the treatment strategies?
This is the causal estimand from Chapter 1.3. Common choices include the intention-to-treat effect, the per-protocol effect, the average treatment effect (ATE), or the restricted mean survival time difference. The choice should be driven by the scientific question, not by what is convenient to estimate.


5.3.8 2.8 Analysis plan
What it means: How will confounding, censoring, and model misspecification be addressed?
The analysis plan specifies the statistical approach: g-computation, inverse probability weighting, TMLE, or another estimator. It also specifies how censoring and loss to follow-up will be handled. This is where the later chapters of this handbook (Chapters 2.1 through 2.5) become relevant.


5.3.9 Summary table: statin initiation example







Protocol component
Target trial specification




Eligibility
Adults 40-75 with new hyperlipidemia diagnosis, no prior CVD event, no prior statin use


Treatment strategies
(A) Initiate atorvastatin within 30 days; (B) No statin initiation within 30 days


Assignment
Random assignment at time of eligibility


Time zero
Date eligibility criteria are first met


Outcome
First major adverse cardiovascular event (MACE): MI, stroke, or CV death


Follow-up
From time zero until MACE, death, loss to follow-up, or 5 years


Causal contrast
Intention-to-treat risk difference at 5 years


Analysis plan
Clone-censor-weight approach for ITT; TMLE with Super Learner for point-treatment emulation
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
section: 5.4 3. Common Biases Prevented by Target Trial Thinking
text:
5.4 3. Common Biases Prevented by Target Trial Thinking
Many well-known biases in observational research can be traced to violations of specific target trial protocol components. Thinking in terms of the protocol makes these biases easier to diagnose and prevent.

5.4.1 3.1 Immortal time bias
Which component is violated? Time zero.
As described in the opening example, immortal time bias occurs when follow-up begins before treatment assignment. The period between the start of follow-up and the treatment event is “immortal” for the treated group (they had to survive to receive treatment). This artificially inflates the survival of the treated group.
Prevention: Align time zero with the moment of treatment assignment. Use a new-user (incident-user) design.


5.4.2 3.2 Prevalent user bias
Which component is violated? Eligibility criteria and time zero.
Prevalent user bias occurs when you include patients who are already on treatment at the start of follow-up. These patients are a selected subset: they have already survived the early period of treatment (when side effects and early failures are most common), and their current covariate values may be consequences of prior treatment rather than confounders.
Prevention: Restrict to new (incident) users who initiate treatment at time zero. This is sometimes called the “new-user design” or “active-comparator, new-user design.”


5.4.3 3.3 Vague comparator definitions
Which component is violated? Treatment strategies.
If the comparator group is “everyone not on the drug,” it includes a mix of people on alternative treatments, people with contraindications, people who will start the drug later, and people who have never been considered for treatment. This creates a poorly defined causal contrast.
Prevention: Define specific, well-characterized treatment strategies. An active comparator (for example, “initiate pravastatin” vs. “initiate atorvastatin”) often leads to a more meaningful and less confounded comparison than a “treated vs. untreated” contrast.


5.4.4 3.4 Post-hoc causal questions
Which component is violated? The entire protocol.
When analysts explore the data first and formulate the causal question afterward, the question is shaped by what the data show rather than by what the science demands. This dramatically inflates the risk of false positive findings.
Prevention: Write the target trial protocol before accessing the data, or at minimum, before running any analyses.






A unifying insight



Nearly every major bias in observational pharmacoepidemiology (immortal time bias, prevalent user bias, selection bias from conditioning on the future, time-window bias) is a violation of one or more target trial protocol components. If you can specify and emulate the protocol correctly, you prevent these biases by design rather than by statistical adjustment.




5.4.5 Simulation: immortal time bias in action
The following simulation demonstrates how failing to align time zero with treatment assignment creates spurious treatment effects. We generate data where the treatment has no causal effect on the outcome, then show that a naive analysis (which misaligns time zero) produces a large apparent benefit.

set.seed(2024)
n <- 5000

# --- Data-generating process (NO treatment effect) ---
# Each person has a baseline hazard for the outcome (death)
# Treatment is initiated at a random time during follow-up (for those who initiate)
baseline_hazard <- 0.10  # annual probability of event
max_follow_up <- 5       # years

sim_data <- tibble(
  id = 1:n,
  # Some patients are "destined" to initiate treatment (60%)
  will_treat = rbinom(n, 1, 0.6),
  # Time to treatment initiation (uniform 0-3 years for those who initiate)
  time_to_treat = ifelse(will_treat == 1, runif(n, 0, 3), Inf),
  # Event time: exponential with rate = baseline_hazard (NO treatment effect)
  event_time = rexp(n, rate = baseline_hazard),
  # Observed event time (censored at max follow-up)
  obs_time = pmin(event_time, max_follow_up),
  event = as.integer(event_time <= max_follow_up),
  # A person actually initiates treatment only if they survive to initiation
  actually_treated = as.integer(will_treat == 1 & time_to_treat < obs_time)
)

# --- WRONG analysis: ignore treatment timing (immortal time bias) ---
# Classify entire follow-up as treated/untreated based on whether they
# ever received treatment during follow-up
wrong_analysis <- sim_data |>
  group_by(actually_treated) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    events = sum(event),
    person_years = sum(obs_time),
    rate = events / person_years,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("=== WRONG analysis (immortal time bias) ===\n")
#> === WRONG analysis (immortal time bias) ===
cat("True treatment effect: NONE (null by construction)\n\n")
#> True treatment effect: NONE (null by construction)
print(as.data.frame(wrong_analysis))
#>   actually_treated    n events person_years       rate
#> 1                0 2405   1197     8122.027 0.14737701
#> 2                1 2595    738    11505.781 0.06414167

rate_ratio_wrong <- wrong_analysis$rate[wrong_analysis$actually_treated == 1] /
  wrong_analysis$rate[wrong_analysis$actually_treated == 0]
cat("\nBiased rate ratio (treated vs untreated):", round(rate_ratio_wrong, 2), "\n")
#> 
#> Biased rate ratio (treated vs untreated): 0.44
cat("Expected if no bias: 1.00\n")
#> Expected if no bias: 1.00

The biased rate ratio is well below 1.0, suggesting that treatment is protective, even though we constructed the data so that treatment has zero causal effect. The entire “benefit” comes from the fact that treated patients had to survive long enough to receive treatment.
Now let us fix the analysis by aligning time zero with treatment initiation, using a new-user design with time-matched controls.

# --- CORRECT analysis: align time zero with treatment initiation ---
# For each treated person, time zero = time of treatment initiation
# Match to an untreated person still at risk at that same time

treated <- sim_data |>
  filter(actually_treated == 1) |>
  mutate(
    t0 = time_to_treat,
    remaining_time = obs_time - t0,
    event_after_t0 = as.integer(event_time > t0 & event_time <= max_follow_up)
  )

# For controls: sample from those still at risk at each treatment time
set.seed(123)
matched_controls <- treated |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(
    # Find a control still alive and untreated at this time
    control_id = {
      eligible <- sim_data |>
        filter(actually_treated == 0, obs_time > t0)
      if (nrow(eligible) > 0) sample(eligible$id, 1) else NA_integer_
    }
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!is.na(control_id))

# Get control outcomes from t0 onward
controls_outcome <- matched_controls |>
  left_join(
    sim_data |> select(id, event_time_ctrl = event_time, obs_time_ctrl = obs_time),
    by = c("control_id" = "id")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    remaining_time_ctrl = obs_time_ctrl - t0,
    event_after_t0_ctrl = as.integer(
      event_time_ctrl > t0 & event_time_ctrl <= max_follow_up
    )
  )

rate_treated <- sum(controls_outcome$event_after_t0) /
  sum(controls_outcome$remaining_time)
rate_control <- sum(controls_outcome$event_after_t0_ctrl) /
  sum(controls_outcome$remaining_time_ctrl)

cat("=== CORRECT analysis (time zero aligned) ===\n")
#> === CORRECT analysis (time zero aligned) ===
cat("Rate in treated (from t0):", round(rate_treated, 3), "\n")
#> Rate in treated (from t0): 0.094
cat("Rate in controls (from t0):", round(rate_control, 3), "\n")
#> Rate in controls (from t0): 0.126
cat("Corrected rate ratio:", round(rate_treated / rate_control, 2), "\n")
#> Corrected rate ratio: 0.75
cat("Expected if no bias: ~1.00\n")
#> Expected if no bias: ~1.00







What the simulation shows



When time zero is misaligned, a null treatment looks highly protective (rate ratio well below 1). When time zero is properly aligned with treatment initiation, the rate ratio is close to 1.0, as it should be. No fancy statistical method can fix a fundamentally flawed study design.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.5 4. Connection to the Causal Roadmap
The target trial protocol and the Causal Roadmap (Chapter 1.2) are complementary frameworks. The target trial protocol makes the causal question precise. The roadmap then provides the systematic workflow for moving from that question to a defensible estimate. The following table shows how each roadmap step maps to target trial components.








Causal Roadmap step
Target trial component(s)
What it accomplishes




Step 1: Define the causal question
Eligibility, treatment strategies, outcome, causal contrast
Specifies the population, interventions, and estimand


Step 2: Specify the causal model
Assignment procedure, eligibility
Encodes assumptions about confounding structure (DAG)


Step 3: State identification assumptions
Assignment procedure, time zero
Justifies exchangeability, positivity, consistency


Step 4: Define the statistical estimand
Causal contrast, analysis plan
Translates the causal estimand into a function of observed data


Step 5: Choose an estimator
Analysis plan
Selects g-computation, IPTW, TMLE, or another method


Step 6: Perform sensitivity analysis
Assignment procedure, analysis plan
Assesses robustness to violations of assumptions









How the pieces fit together



The target trial protocol (this chapter) gives you Step 1. Identification (Chapter 1.3) gives you Steps 2 through 4. The estimation chapters (Chapters 2.1 through 2.5) give you Step 5. Together, they form a complete workflow for drawing causal conclusions from observational data.


Notice how time zero alignment maps to the identification assumptions. If time zero is misaligned, the consistency assumption is violated: the potential outcome \(Y(a)\) is not well-defined when treatment assignment and follow-up start at different times. Similarly, if prevalent users are included, the exchangeability assumption is threatened because current covariates may be affected by prior treatment.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
section: 5.6 5. Worked Example: Statin Initiation and MACE
text:
5.6 5. Worked Example: Statin Initiation and MACE
We now walk through a complete target trial emulation using simulated registry-style data. The scientific question is: Does initiating a statin (vs. not initiating) reduce 3-year MACE risk among adults with new hyperlipidemia?

5.6.1 Step 1: Define the target trial protocol







Component
Specification




Eligibility
Age 40-75, new hyperlipidemia diagnosis, no prior CVD, no prior statin use


Treatment strategies
(A) Initiate statin within 30 days; (B) No statin initiation within 30 days


Assignment
Randomized at eligibility date


Time zero
Date eligibility criteria first met


Outcome
First MACE (MI, stroke, or CV death)


Follow-up
3 years from time zero


Causal contrast
ATE on the risk difference scale


Analysis plan
G-computation with logistic regression; sensitivity analysis with IPTW





5.6.2 Step 2: Simulate registry data and construct the new-user cohort

set.seed(42)
n <- 3000

# --- Simulate baseline covariates at time of eligibility ---
cohort <- tibble(
  id = 1:n,
  age = round(runif(n, 40, 75)),
  male = rbinom(n, 1, 0.52),
  diabetes = rbinom(n, 1, 0.25),
  smoking = rbinom(n, 1, 0.20),
  ldl = round(rnorm(n, mean = 160, sd = 30)),   # mg/dL at diagnosis
  sbp = round(rnorm(n, mean = 135, sd = 18))     # systolic BP at diagnosis
)

# --- Treatment assignment (confounded by covariates) ---
# Sicker patients more likely to receive statins
cohort <- cohort |>
  mutate(
    lp_treat = -1.5 + 0.02 * (age - 55) + 0.3 * male + 0.5 * diabetes +
      0.3 * smoking + 0.01 * (ldl - 160) + 0.01 * (sbp - 135),
    pr_treat = plogis(lp_treat),
    A = rbinom(n, 1, pr_treat)
  )

# --- Outcome: 3-year MACE risk ---
# True causal effect: statin reduces MACE risk (risk difference ~ -0.05)
cohort <- cohort |>
  mutate(
    lp_outcome = -2.5 - 0.4 * A + 0.03 * (age - 55) + 0.2 * male +
      0.6 * diabetes + 0.4 * smoking + 0.005 * (ldl - 160) +
      0.01 * (sbp - 135),
    pr_mace = plogis(lp_outcome),
    Y = rbinom(n, 1, pr_mace)
  )

# Verify confounding: crude vs. true effect
crude_rd <- mean(cohort$Y[cohort$A == 1]) - mean(cohort$Y[cohort$A == 0])
cat("Crude risk difference:", round(crude_rd, 4), "\n")
#> Crude risk difference: -0.0362
cat("(Confounded because sicker patients are more likely to get statins)\n")
#> (Confounded because sicker patients are more likely to get statins)



5.6.3 Step 3: Time-zero alignment
In this simulated dataset, time zero is the date of eligibility (new hyperlipidemia diagnosis). Treatment assignment (A) is determined at time zero. Follow-up begins at time zero. All three conditions for proper time-zero alignment are satisfied by construction.
In real data, you would need to verify this carefully:

# Verify time-zero alignment
# In a real analysis, you would check:
cohort_check <- cohort |>
  summarise(
    n_total = n(),
    n_treated = sum(A),
    n_control = sum(1 - A),
    prop_treated = mean(A),
    outcome_rate_treated = mean(Y[A == 1]),
    outcome_rate_control = mean(Y[A == 0])
  )

cat("=== New-user cohort summary ===\n")
#> === New-user cohort summary ===
cat("Total eligible patients:", cohort_check$n_total, "\n")
#> Total eligible patients: 3000
cat("Statin initiators:", cohort_check$n_treated,
    sprintf("(%.1f%%)", 100 * cohort_check$prop_treated), "\n")
#> Statin initiators: 778 (25.9%)
cat("Non-initiators:", cohort_check$n_control, "\n")
#> Non-initiators: 2222
cat("3-year MACE rate (treated):",
    sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * cohort_check$outcome_rate_treated), "\n")
#> 3-year MACE rate (treated): 8.5%
cat("3-year MACE rate (untreated):",
    sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * cohort_check$outcome_rate_control), "\n")
#> 3-year MACE rate (untreated): 12.1%



5.6.4 Step 4: Estimation via g-computation
Following Chapter 2.1, we use g-computation to estimate the average treatment effect while adjusting for confounders.

# --- G-computation ---
# Step 1: Fit outcome model
outcome_model <- glm(
  Y ~ A + age + male + diabetes + smoking + ldl + sbp,
  data = cohort,
  family = binomial
)

# Step 2: Predict potential outcomes under each treatment strategy
cohort_gcomp <- cohort |>
  mutate(
    # Predicted probability if everyone received statins
    Y1_hat = predict(outcome_model,
                     newdata = mutate(cohort, A = 1),
                     type = "response"),
    # Predicted probability if nobody received statins
    Y0_hat = predict(outcome_model,
                     newdata = mutate(cohort, A = 0),
                     type = "response")
  )

# Step 3: Compute the ATE (marginal risk difference)
ate_gcomp <- mean(cohort_gcomp$Y1_hat) - mean(cohort_gcomp$Y0_hat)
risk_treated <- mean(cohort_gcomp$Y1_hat)
risk_untreated <- mean(cohort_gcomp$Y0_hat)

cat("=== G-computation results ===\n")
#> === G-computation results ===
cat("Estimated 3-year MACE risk under statin initiation:",
    sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * risk_treated), "\n")
#> Estimated 3-year MACE risk under statin initiation: 7.4%
cat("Estimated 3-year MACE risk under no initiation:   ",
    sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * risk_untreated), "\n")
#> Estimated 3-year MACE risk under no initiation:    12.7%
cat("Estimated ATE (risk difference):",
    sprintf("%.4f", ate_gcomp),
    sprintf("(%.1f percentage points)", 100 * ate_gcomp), "\n")
#> Estimated ATE (risk difference): -0.0536 (-5.4 percentage points)
cat("\nInterpretation: Initiating statins is estimated to",
    ifelse(ate_gcomp < 0, "reduce", "increase"),
    "3-year MACE risk by",
    sprintf("%.1f", abs(100 * ate_gcomp)),
    "percentage points.\n")
#> 
#> Interpretation: Initiating statins is estimated to reduce 3-year MACE risk by 5.4 percentage points.



5.6.5 Step 5: Bootstrap confidence interval

# --- Bootstrap inference ---
set.seed(99)
n_boot <- 500
boot_ates <- numeric(n_boot)

for (b in seq_len(n_boot)) {
  boot_idx <- sample(nrow(cohort), replace = TRUE)
  boot_data <- cohort[boot_idx, ]

  boot_model <- glm(
    Y ~ A + age + male + diabetes + smoking + ldl + sbp,
    data = boot_data,
    family = binomial
  )

  p1 <- predict(boot_model, newdata = mutate(boot_data, A = 1), type = "response")
  p0 <- predict(boot_model, newdata = mutate(boot_data, A = 0), type = "response")
  boot_ates[b] <- mean(p1) - mean(p0)
}

ci <- quantile(boot_ates, probs = c(0.025, 0.975))

cat("=== Target trial emulation: final results ===\n")
#> === Target trial emulation: final results ===
cat("Estimand: 3-year ATE (risk difference), intention-to-treat\n")
#> Estimand: 3-year ATE (risk difference), intention-to-treat
cat("Method: G-computation with logistic regression\n")
#> Method: G-computation with logistic regression
cat("Point estimate:", sprintf("%.4f", ate_gcomp), "\n")
#> Point estimate: -0.0536
cat("95% bootstrap CI: [", sprintf("%.4f", ci[1]), ",",
    sprintf("%.4f", ci[2]), "]\n")
#> 95% bootstrap CI: [ -0.0773 , -0.0292 ]



5.6.6 Step 6: Interpret in the target trial framework






Interpreting the results



The estimated risk difference should be reported in the language of the target trial. For example: “In a hypothetical trial where adults aged 40 to 75 with new hyperlipidemia were randomized to initiate atorvastatin vs. no statin, the estimated 3-year MACE risk difference is [point estimate] (95% CI: [lower, upper]).”
This framing makes the causal claim explicit, reminds the reader of the assumptions required (exchangeability given measured covariates, positivity, consistency), and connects the statistical result back to the scientific question.


The validity of this analysis rests on the target trial protocol being correctly specified and emulated:

Time zero is aligned: eligibility, treatment assignment, and follow-up all start at diagnosis.
New-user design prevents prevalent user bias: no one is already on a statin at time zero.
Treatment strategies are specific: initiate vs. do not initiate within 30 days.
Confounding adjustment relies on the assumption that age, sex, diabetes, smoking, LDL, and SBP are sufficient to achieve conditional exchangeability (Chapter 1.3). In a real study, you would use a DAG to justify this assumption and perform sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounding.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.7 Summary






Key takeaways




Start with the protocol, not the data. Every observational causal analysis should begin by specifying the target trial you wish you could run. The eight protocol components (eligibility, treatment strategies, assignment, time zero, outcome, follow-up, causal contrast, and analysis plan) make the causal question unambiguous.
Time zero alignment is the most common source of serious bias. When eligibility, treatment assignment, and follow-up start do not coincide, biases such as immortal time bias are introduced. These biases can be large enough to fully explain an observed treatment effect, and they cannot be corrected with statistical adjustment.
New-user designs prevent prevalent user bias. Restricting to patients who are initiating (not continuing) treatment ensures that baseline covariates are not affected by prior treatment and that early treatment effects are captured.
The target trial protocol is Step 1 of the Causal Roadmap. It defines the causal question. Identification assumptions (Chapter 1.3), estimation methods (Chapters 2.1 through 2.5), and diagnostics follow from there.
Document every deviation. When the observational emulation differs from the ideal trial (as it always will), document each difference and assess how it might introduce bias. Transparency about limitations is more valuable than false precision.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.8 Check your understanding


Q1. A researcher defines “statin users” as anyone who filled a statin prescription at any time during a 5-year observation window, and compares their outcomes to people who never filled a prescription. What bias is most likely, and which target trial component was violated?

This design introduces immortal time bias. The violated component is time zero. Statin users are classified based on a future event (filling a prescription at some point during follow-up), and their follow-up is counted from a time before treatment actually began. The period between the start of observation and the prescription fill date is “immortal” for the treated group, because dying before the fill date would have moved them to the untreated group. This creates a spurious survival advantage for treated patients. The fix is to align time zero with the date of statin initiation and only compare to controls who are still at risk at that same time.



Q2. Why is a “new-user” (incident-user) design preferred over including patients already on treatment? Explain in terms of target trial components.

Including patients already on treatment (prevalent users) violates the eligibility criteria and time zero components of the target trial. Prevalent users are a selected group: they have already survived the initiation period, tolerated the drug, and chosen to continue it. Their current covariate values (such as LDL cholesterol or blood pressure) may be consequences of prior treatment rather than pre-treatment confounders. Adjusting for covariates affected by treatment introduces collider bias (or “overadjustment”). A new-user design avoids these problems by starting follow-up at the moment of treatment initiation, just as a trial would enroll patients at the point of randomization. This ensures that baseline covariates are measured before treatment begins and that early events are captured.



Q3. How does the target trial protocol connect to the identification assumptions discussed in Chapter 1.3? Give one example of a protocol component that directly supports one identification assumption.

The target trial protocol makes identification assumptions concrete and verifiable. For example, the assignment procedure component directly supports the exchangeability (no unmeasured confounding) assumption. In the target trial, randomization guarantees exchangeability. In the emulation, we must argue that, conditional on measured covariates, treatment assignment is independent of potential outcomes. The target trial protocol forces us to explicitly specify what confounders we are adjusting for and why, connecting the design to the identification assumptions.
Another example: the treatment strategies component supports the consistency assumption. Well-defined treatment strategies ensure that “treatment” means the same thing for everyone, so the potential outcome \(Y(a)\) is well-defined. Vague strategies (“statin use”) can violate consistency because different patients may experience very different treatment versions (different drugs, doses, or durations).
A third example: the eligibility criteria and the scope of the target population help support the positivity assumption. By defining the population carefully, we can ensure that within every stratum of confounders, there are both treated and untreated individuals.
crumbs: Part I: Foundations
5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence

title: 5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
text:
5.9 What comes next
With the target trial protocol defining our causal question (this chapter), the identification framework linking causal and statistical estimands (Chapter 1.3), and the Causal Roadmap organizing the workflow (Chapter 1.2), we are ready to move from design to estimation. In the next part of the handbook, we will learn the statistical methods that deliver on the promise of this framework, beginning with g-computation (Chapter 2.1), inverse probability weighting (Chapter 2.2), and the doubly robust TMLE estimator (Chapter 2.3).

5  Chapter 1.4: Target Trial Emulation for Real-World Evidence
